title: Quiz: Generalization Bounds
description: Test your understanding of PAC learning and sample complexity in QML

questions.question: What does PAC learning guarantee?

options.text: Error <= epsilon with probability >= 1-delta using polynomial samples

questions.explanation: PAC (Probably Approximately Correct) learning guarantees that with enough samples (polynomial in 1/epsilon, 1/delta), the learned hypothesis has error at most epsilon with probability at least 1-delta.

questions.question: What does Rademacher complexity measure?

options.text: The computational complexity of the model
The ability of a function class to fit random labels
The number of parameters in the model
The entanglement in the quantum circuit

questions.explanation: Rademacher complexity measures how well a function class can correlate with random +/-1 labels. Higher complexity means the class can fit more random patterns, indicating potential for overfitting.

questions.question: How does sample complexity scale with VC dimension d?
questions.explanation: Sample complexity scales as O((d + log(1/delta)) / epsilon^2). This means linearly in VC dimension and quadratically in the inverse of the target accuracy.

questions.question: What is the generalization gap?

options.text: The difference between quantum and classical model accuracy
The difference between test error and training error

questions.explanation: The generalization gap is test error minus training error. It measures how well the model performs on unseen data compared to training data. Large gaps indicate overfitting.

questions.question: Quantum models require fewer samples than classical models with the same number of parameters.
questions.explanation: False. Sample complexity scales with effective model complexity (like VC dimension), which is similar for quantum and classical models with the same parameter count. The quantum advantage comes from expressiveness, not sample efficiency.

questions.question: What does VC dimension characterize?

options.text: The largest set that can be shattered (all labelings realized)

questions.explanation: VC dimension is the size of the largest set S such that the hypothesis class can realize all 2^|S| possible labelings. It measures the capacity of the hypothesis class.

questions.question: For a quantum circuit with L layers and n qubits, what is the approximate VC dimension?
questions.explanation: The VC dimension of quantum circuits scales approximately linearly with the number of parameters, which is O(L * n). The exponential Hilbert space affects expressiveness but not sample complexity.

questions.question: Which factors affect the generalization bound? (Select all that apply)
questions.explanation: Generalization bounds depend on sample size (more samples = tighter bound), model complexity (simpler models generalize better), and confidence delta (higher confidence = looser bound). Physical properties of hardware don't affect theoretical bounds.
